metadata.title: Why a Perfectly Calibrated Model Can Be Perfectly Useless
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Computational Skepticism for AI candidate 22. Source: books/computational-skepticism-for-ai/chapters/11-communicating-uncertainty-calibrating-claims-to-evidence.md (calibration vs discrimination, base-rate predictor, lines 140, 304).
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = good calibration / on the diagonal; CRIMSON = fails to discriminate / useless for decisions; GOLD = the key insight / the two axes; SLATE = base rate / constant prediction
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no Brier score formula or three-term decomposition algebra, no ECE binning math, no AUROC definition, no temperature scaling — the two dot-patterns carry everything
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B15: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A perfectly calibrated model. Completely useless.

card.copy:
Why a Perfectly Calibrated Model
Can Be Perfectly Useless

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. A perfectly calibrated model. Completely useless.

beats.narration_text: Here is a medical AI model. Its calibration score is exactly zero — the best possible. When it says sixty percent, it's right sixty percent of the time. When it says thirty percent, thirty percent of cases are positive. Perfect. And for every single patient, it gives the same answer: the base rate. Completely useless for any individual decision.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Here is a medical AI model. Its calibration score is exactly zero — the best possible. When it says sixty percent, it's right sixty percent of the time. When it

beats.narration_text: If a model is perfectly calibrated — stating confidence that matches reality exactly — how can it be completely useless?

props.headline: If a model is perfectly calibrated — stating confidence that matches…

card.copy:
If the model is perfectly calibrated —
how can it be completely useless?

beats.narration_text: Calibration measures one thing: does the model's stated confidence match observed frequency? A model that always says 'thirty percent' — the base rate — is perfectly calibrated. Thirty percent of cases are positive. It said thirty percent. It was right. Every time.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Calibration measures one thing: does the model's stated confidence match observed frequency? A model that always says 'thirty percent' — the base rate — is perf

beats.narration_text: But calibration doesn't measure whether the model's output is useful for your decision. Useful means different things for different patients — high risk versus low, treat now versus wait. A model that gives everyone the same number cannot help you tell them apart. That's discrimination — and calibration is blind to it.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: But calibration doesn't measure whether the model's output is useful for your decision. Useful means different things for different patients — high risk versus

beats.narration_text: Honesty and usefulness are separate axes.

props.headline: Honesty and usefulness are separate axes.

beats.image_description: A calibration reliability diagram showing a perfectly straight diagonal line from lower-left to upper-right, but all the prediction dots clustered at a single point near the center

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Honesty and usefulness are separate axes.

beats.narration_text: On the reliability diagram, perfect calibration means your dots fall on the diagonal. But if every patient gets the same prediction — the base rate — all your dots pile onto one point. You're on the diagonal, exactly. ECE of zero. And the diagram reveals nothing about whether any patient should be treated differently from any other.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: On the reliability diagram, perfect calibration means your dots fall on the diagonal. But if every patient gets the same prediction — the base rate — all your d

beats.narration_text: A model with perfect calibration but no resolution — predicting the base rate for every instance — has a calibration error of zero and is completely useless for triage.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A model with perfect calibration but no resolution — predicting the base rate for every instance — has a calibration error of zero and is completely useless for

beats.narration_text: What you need is both. Calibration: when you say sixty percent, you mean sixty percent. Discrimination: you say something different for the sixty-percent patient than for the ten-percent patient. A single aggregate metric collapses both into one number — and lets you miss the failure you actually have.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: What you need is both. Calibration: when you say sixty percent, you mean sixty percent. Discrimination: you say something different for the sixty-percent patien

beats.narration_text: A model that is well-calibrated but predicts close to the base rate for every instance is honest about uncertainty but useless for triage.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A model that is well-calibrated but predicts close to the base rate for every instance is honest about uncertainty but useless for triage.

beats.narration_text: When you report a calibration score, check whether it is masking a discrimination failure. Plot the reliability diagram. Check whether predictions spread across the range — or pile on the base rate. The aggregate metric is evidence the model is calibrated on average. It is not evidence the model is useful for any individual case.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: When you report a calibration score, check whether it is masking a discrimination failure. Plot the reliability diagram. Check whether predictions spread across

beats.narration_text: A bank deploys a fraud detection model. Calibration score: perfect. ECE of zero. The fraud team runs it for a week. Every transaction gets the same output: two-point-one percent — the base rate. A five-dollar coffee purchase: two-point-one. A ten-thousand-dollar international wire: two-point-one. The team cannot flag anything useful, because the model has nothing to say about any individual transaction. It is perfectly honest. It is completely useless.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A bank deploys a fraud detection model. Calibration score: perfect. ECE of zero. The fraud team runs it for a week. Every transaction gets the same output: two-

beats.narration_text: Calibration measures honesty — does the stated confidence match reality. Discrimination measures usefulness — does the model say different things for different cases. A perfect score on one axis is no evidence of the other.

props.headline: Calibration measures honesty — does the stated confidence match reality. Discrimination…

card.copy:
Calibration = honesty.
Discrimination = usefulness.
They are separate axes.
card.sub: A perfect calibration score can hide a model that's useless for every individual case.

beats.name: OutroSeries
